title: BLW Introduction Chart 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Check if your baby is ready for baby-led weaning. Learn signs of readiness and safe first foods for 6+ months.
intro: Parenting and family wellness require planning across multiple dimensions: health, nutrition, safety, and developmental milestones. Each stage—from pregnancy through infancy, childhood, and adolescence—has specific milestones, safety considerations, and feeding guidelines. Introducing solid foods is a major decision. Whether you choose baby-led weaning (BLW) or traditional spoon-feeding, starting at the right time with appropriate foods matters for nutrition and safety. Values and guidance are updated to 2026.
keyTakeaway: **6+ months with food strips**: Check readiness signs first. Direct calculation based on your baby's age. Introduction to solid foods for babies starting BLW.
useCases: BLW readiness calculations and solid food introduction planning
Parents evaluating when to start their baby on solids
Quick check of BLW guidelines before introducing foods
explanation:
**Family and parenting** involve health, nutrition, safety, and education. Each developmental stage has specific milestones, readiness signs, and feeding guidelines.

## How It's Calculated

This calculator checks your baby's age and readiness signs for solid food introduction, verifying they fall within safe developmental ranges and alerting you if values seem unusual.

## Important Notes

This calculator is an informational reference only. For medical, nutritional, or safety decisions, always consult with your pediatrician or a feeding specialist. Guidelines and recommendations are updated to 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: Baby-led weaning is an approach where babies self-feed soft foods and finger foods starting around 6 months, rather than eating purees from a spoon. It supports independence and natural appetite regulation.

faq.q: When can my baby start BLW?
faq.a: Most babies are ready around 6 months of age. Look for readiness signs: sitting upright without support, showing interest in food, and developing the pincer grasp (picking things up with thumb and fingers).

faq.q: What are the signs my baby is ready for solids?
faq.a: Key signs include: sitting up with minimal support, loss of tongue-thrust reflex (food stays in mouth), good head control, and reaching for food on your plate.

faq.q: What are the best first foods for BLW?
faq.a: Start with soft, easily mashable foods in strip or finger-sized pieces: cooked sweet potato, banana, avocado, soft cooked broccoli, cooked carrot, and well-cooked pasta.

faq.q: What foods should I avoid when starting BLW?
faq.a: Avoid whole nuts, whole grapes, whole cherry tomatoes, honey (before age 1), hard raw vegetables, popcorn, and choking hazards. Always supervise eating and know the difference between gagging and choking.

faq.q: Is gagging the same as choking?
faq.a: No. Gagging is a protective reflex—baby coughs, makes noise, and clears the food. Choking is silent and dangerous. Know both signs and when to seek help.

faq.q: Will my baby get enough nutrition from finger foods?
faq.a: Breast milk or formula remains the primary nutrition source until 12 months. Solids are for exploration and gradual nutritional contribution. Most intake still comes from milk feeds.

faq.q: Should I add salt or sugar to baby foods?
faq.a: No. Avoid adding salt, sugar, honey (before 1 year), or seasoning to baby foods during the first year. Let babies taste natural flavors of foods.
